title: Henry Corbin on the Mundus Imaginalis and the Guide of Light
subtitle: Expanded with source passages from The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism

summary.one_line: Corbin's mundus imaginalis ('alam al-mithal) as the intermediate ontological region between matter and spirit, perceived by the active Imagination, and the manifestation of the Guide of light (Perf...
summary.scope: Primary source: Man of Light in Iranian Sufism (Corbin, 1978). Secondary: Adventures in the Imaginal, Apophasis and Angelology.

sources.label: Henry Corbin — The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism (Omega, 1994)

body.text: Between the world of pure spiritual Lights (Luces victoriales, the world of the 'Mothers' in the terminology of Ishraq) and the sensory universe, at the boundary of the ninth Sphere (the Sphere of Spheres) there opens a mundus imaginalis which is a concrete spiritual world of archetype-Figures, apparitional Forms, Angels of species and of individuals; by philosophical dialectics its necessity is deduced and its plane situated; vision of it in actuality is vouchsafed to the visionary apperception of the active Imagination.
body.notes: Core definition of the imaginal world as intermediate ontological region

body.text: This suprasensory Orient governs the primary phenomenon of the Gnostic's orientation toward his country of origin. The Orient-origin identified with the center, with the heavenly north pole, heralds access to the beyond, where vision becomes real history, the history of the soul, and where every visionary event symbolizes a spiritual state; or, as the Ishraqiyun say, it is the climate 'where what is bodily becomes spirit and what is spiritual acquires a body.'
body.notes: The climate of the Soul where matter and spirit interpenetrate

body.text: It is the heavenly entity, the philosopher's Angel, conjoined with his star, which rules him and opens the doors of wisdom for him, teaches him what is difficult, reveals to him what is right, in sleeping as in waking.
body.notes: Classic Hermetic definition of Perfect Nature from the Ghayat al-Hakim

body.text: When I wished to bring to light the science of the mystery and modality of Creation, I came upon a subterranean vault filled with darkness and winds... Lo and behold, a person then appeared to me in my sleep in a form of the greatest beauty. He said to me: 'Take a lamp and place it under a glass to shield it from the winds; then it will give thee light in spite of them...' At that I said, 'Who then art thou?' He answered: 'I am thy Perfect Nature. If thou wishest to see me, call me by my name.'
body.notes: Hermes' visionary encounter with Perfect Nature — the classic initiatic narrative

body.text: Having cut in two the thread spun by a spider, the prince puts it together again, saying: 1 x 1. This is also the formula that we suggested above, because he who deciphers it holds the key to the secret that preserves him both from pseudomystical monism (whose formula is 1 = 1) and from abstract monotheism which is content to superimpose an Ens supremum on the multitude of beings (n + 1).
body.notes: The 1x1 formula as the cipher of the union of Perfect Nature and the man of light

body.text: My image looks at me with my own look; I look at it with its own look. Their relation is this unus-ambo in which each of the two simultaneously assumes the position of the I and the self — image and mirror.
body.notes: The mirror-gaze reciprocity that defines the unus-ambo

body.text: When the circle of the face has become pure, it effuses lights as a spring pours forth its water... Before you, before your face, there is another Face also of light, irradiating lights... In reality this Face is your own face and this sun is the sun of the Spirit (shams al-ruh) that goes to and fro in your body. Next, the whole of your person is immersed in purity, and suddenly you are gazing at a person of light (shakhs nurani) who is also irradiating lights... This person of light before you is called in Sufi terminology the suprasensory Guide (moqaddam al-ghayb).
body.notes: Najm Kobra's description of the vision of the heavenly Witness

body.text: Of all spiritual practices... the dhikr is the practice most apt to free spiritual energy, that is, to allow the particle of divine light which is in the mystic to rejoin its like... The fire of the dhikr is visualized as a pure and ardent blaze, animated by a rapid upward movement... It sets fire to all that is there to be consumed, and sheds light on any darkness it may encounter. If light is there already, the two lights associate with each other and there is light upon light.
body.notes: The dhikr as the mechanism for freeing the captive light

body.text: Green is the color that outlasts the others. From this color emanate flashing, sparkling rays... the heart is a subtle organ which reflects suprasensory things and realities that revolve around it... the heart is a light in the depths of the well of nature, like Joseph's light in the well into which he was thrown.
body.notes: The emerald vision as the sign of the awakened heart

body.text: The suprasensory Witness, the suprasensory Guide, the suprasensory Scales: this is what you are shown when you close your eyelids. According to whether what appears to you is light or darkness, your witness (shahid) is light or darkness.
body.notes: The Witness as the measure of the soul's state

body.text: Then who art thou, whose beauty outshines all other beauty ever contemplated in the terrestrial world? I am thine own Daena. I was loved, thou hast made me more loved still. I was beautiful, thou hast made me still more beautiful.
body.notes: The Zoroastrian prototype of the Guide encounter at the Chinvat Bridge

body.text: What we are speaking of is a counterpart, a celestial, transcendent counterpart, rather than a 'double'; the idea of this heavenly partner is antithetic to the Double... psychological analysis shows this Double to be the manifestation of the personal unconscious, hence belonging to the functions of the lower psyche, that is, the nafs ammara, the dark envelope, the shadow, exactly what the dhikr has to destroy by fire so that the Guide of light may become visible.
body.notes: Critical distinction: the Guide is not the Doppelganger

body.text: Thou, my lord and prince, my most holy angel, my precious spiritual being, Thou art the Spirit who gave birth to me, and Thou art the Child to whom my spirit gives birth... Thou who art clothed in the most brilliant of divine Lights... may Thou manifest Thyself to me in the most beautiful of epiphanies, show me the light of Thy dazzling face, be for me the mediator... lift the veils of darkness from my heart...
body.notes: Sohravardi's psalm to Perfect Nature — the most beautiful prayer to the Angel

body.text: The 'eighth' keshvar, the mystical Earth of Hurqalya with emerald cities; it is situated on the summit of the cosmic mountain, which the traditions handed down in Islam call the mountain of Qaf. This is the paradisal Earth of Light, the world of Hurqalya, an Orient intermediate between the 'lesser Orient,' which is the soul's rising to the highest point of its desire and consciousness, and the 'greater Orient,' which is the further spiritual Orient, the pleroma of pure Intelligences.
body.notes: The cosmic north as the threshold of the beyond

body.text: The growth of the subtle organism, the physiology of the man of light, progresses through the seven latifa, each of which is one of the seven prophets of your being; the cycle of birth and initiatic growth is homologous to the cycle of prophecy.
body.notes: Semnani's doctrine of the seven subtle organs corresponding to the seven prophets

body.text: The black light is the light of the pure Essence in its ipseity, in its abscondity; the ability to perceive it depends on a spiritual state described as 'reabsorption in God' (fana fi'llah)... The perfection of contingent being is to regress to its basic negativity, and to come to know through its own unknowingness. It means to know with the certainty of experience that the summum of knowledge is unknowingness, for here there is infinite disproportion.
body.notes: The black light as the inaccessible divine Ipseity

body.text: There is a difference in that the visionary apperception presupposes the opening of the inner eye by the removal of the veil which darkened it, whereas the feeling of mystic experience (dhawq) is caused by a transmutation of the being and of the spirit... The five senses are changed into other senses.
body.notes: The transformation of the senses into suprasensory organs of the man of light

body.text: I fell passionately in love with a young girl... the flame of love within me became extraordinarily intense. My breath exhaled flames of fire. And each time I breathed out fire, lo and behold, from the height of heaven someone was also breathing out fire which came to meet my own breath... at last I understood that it was my witness in Heaven.
body.notes: Najm Kobra's personal account of recognizing his heavenly Witness through the fire of love

gaps: Suhrawardi's own writings on Perfect Nature

issues.title: Wolfson paper not included
issues.description: Wolfson on mundus imaginalis available but not extracted.

issues.title: Comparative analysis needed
issues.description: Full cross-traditional comparison with Steiner and Tantraloka not yet incorporated into RO passages.

versions.summary: Expanded with 18 source passages from Corbin's Man of Light in Iranian Sufism

timeline.event: RO expanded with Man of Light passages
